title: The Subway

jokes.text: Opening monologue about the helpless kid stuck in bumper cars needing attendant help
jokes.setup: Everyone remembers bumper cars from childhood
jokes.punchline: That one kid who couldn't do it, stuck crying while attendant helps with big pole
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld observational comedy with vivid physical imagery. Well-structured with clear setup and escalation.

jokes.text: The helpless father-and-son bumper car team who can't organize
jokes.punchline: Unorganized duo arguing 'Who's on the wheel? Who's pressing gas?' while going nowhere
jokes.explanation: Solid follow-up to the bumper car bit with relatable family dynamics.

jokes.text: Overly complicated subway directions to Coney Island
jokes.setup: Jerry giving detailed subway route instructions
jokes.punchline: After all the complex transfers, someone points out he could just take the D straight there
jokes.explanation: Good setup/payoff structure lampooning NYC subway complexity and overthinking.

jokes.text: Don't whistle on the elevator - that's what Willy Loman told Biff in Death of a Salesman
jokes.punchline: Obscure literary reference followed by George's horror at being compared to literature's biggest loser
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment showing Jerry's literary pretensions and George's neuroses. Smart reference that works on multiple levels.

jokes.text: How do they work the bride and groom on that? Do they flip a coin?
jokes.punchline: George's tactless question about wedding roles
jokes.explanation: Typical George tactlessness, well-delivered with his defensive follow-up.

jokes.text: I feel when lesbians are looking at me, they're thinking: 'That's why I'm not a heterosexual.'
jokes.punchline: Her paranoid interpretation of how lesbians perceive her
jokes.explanation: Sharp, quotable line that perfectly captures Elaine's neurotic self-awareness. Economical and character-specific.

jokes.text: List of Kramer's driving violations including 'no doors'
jokes.punchline: Escalating to increasingly absurd violations, culminating in 'no doors'
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalating absurdity that's completely in character for Kramer. The 'no doors' detail is brilliantly specific.

jokes.text: Not only did they find it, it was simonized, and the front end was aligned
jokes.punchline: The thieves actually improved the car
jokes.explanation: Great ironic twist on car theft expectations. Clean setup and surprising payoff.

jokes.text: Who are you, Satan? I'm close to a job here. It's my second interview.
jokes.setup: Kramer trying to convince George to go to Coney Island
jokes.punchline: George's dramatic overreaction comparing Kramer to Satan
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing George's paranoia about job prospects and tendency to dramatize.

jokes.text: All right, Biff.
jokes.setup: George refusing to go to Coney Island because of job interview
jokes.punchline: Jerry calling back to the Death of a Salesman reference
jokes.explanation: Effective callback that reinforces the literary reference and Jerry's needling of George.

jokes.text: You looking for a job?
jokes.punchline: George's increasingly elaborate lies about being successful in 'the market'
jokes.explanation: Sets up George's con perfectly with his characteristic lying and bluffing.

jokes.text: Which market? The... The big one. The big market with the big board. Bull market, bear market. You name the market, I'm there.
jokes.setup: Woman asks which market George works in
jokes.punchline: George's panicked improvisation with stock market clichés
jokes.explanation: Perfect George moment showing his panic-driven bullshitting. The repetitive 'big' structure is comedically effective.

jokes.text: Big brokerage houses killed my father. Well, they hurt him bad. Really hurt his feelings.
jokes.setup: George dramatically claims brokerage houses killed his father
jokes.punchline: Immediate backtrack to 'hurt his feelings'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection that perfectly captures George's tendency to exaggerate then backpedal. Great structure and timing.

jokes.text: No, I mean, what does 'ironic' mean?
jokes.setup: Man uses word 'ironic' to describe societal changes
jokes.punchline: Reveals he doesn't actually know what the word means
jokes.explanation: Smart meta-joke about misuse of 'ironic' delivered with perfect deadpan timing.

jokes.text: There is no 'he.' ... It's a lesbian wedding.
jokes.punchline: Awkward revelation about lesbian wedding to conservative elderly man
jokes.explanation: Solid cringe moment building cultural tension between characters from different generations.

jokes.text: I'm the best man.
jokes.punchline: Elaine is the 'best man' at a wedding with no men
jokes.explanation: Nice ironic twist on wedding traditions, economically delivered.

jokes.text: My luck. I don't talk to a soul on the subway for 35 years, I get the best man at a lesbian wedding.
jokes.setup: Man's isolation on subway for decades
jokes.punchline: Of all people to finally talk to, it's someone involved in lesbian wedding
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about NYC subway culture and generational differences.

jokes.text: I'm not a lesbian! I hate men, but I'm not a lesbian.
jokes.punchline: Elaine's clarification that creates another comedic contradiction
jokes.explanation: Classic Elaine line that perfectly captures her romantic frustrations with characteristic bluntness.

jokes.text: Hey, what's another million, give or take? I get off when and where I wanna get off.
jokes.setup: George being tempted to get off train with attractive woman
jokes.punchline: His attempt to sound like a high-powered businessman
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing George's false bravado and poor decision-making.

jokes.text: His father was a mudder. His mother was a mudder. His mother was a mudder? What did I say?
jokes.punchline: Repetitive 'mudder' talk with confused reaction
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay joke that becomes funnier through repetition and Kramer's confused delivery.

jokes.text: You realize, of course, you're naked.
jokes.punchline: Matter-of-fact observation of obvious situation
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective deadpan delivery of obvious observation in absurd situation.

jokes.text: Naked, dressed, I don't see any difference.
jokes.punchline: Man's completely casual attitude about public nudity
jokes.explanation: Good character moment establishing the naked man's bizarre nonchalance.

jokes.text: George's extended panic monologue about being trapped on the subway
jokes.punchline: George's neurotic spiral including concentration camp and hostage comparisons
jokes.explanation: Perfect George neurotic breakdown with characteristic overthinking and historical comparisons.

jokes.text: George's internal monologue about 'make yourself comfortable'
jokes.setup: Woman tells George to make himself comfortable
jokes.punchline: George's overthinking every possible interpretation
jokes.explanation: Classic George overthinking scenario that perfectly captures his sexual neuroses and indecision.

jokes.text: Extended Mets analysis between Kramer and naked man
jokes.punchline: One of them is completely naked while having normal sports conversation
jokes.explanation: Absurdist humor from the contrast between normal conversation and bizarre circumstances.

jokes.text: If they win it this year, I'll sit naked with you at the World Series.
jokes.punchline: Kramer's bizarre bet involving public nakedness
jokes.explanation: Perfectly absurd Kramer moment that escalates the naked man situation into betting territory.

jokes.text: Elaine's banana counting to pass time
jokes.punchline: Childish counting method to cope with situation
jokes.explanation: Mild character moment showing Elaine's regression under stress.

jokes.text: My mother used to walk around our apartment in her bra and panties. She didn't look anything like you. She was really disgusting, really bad body... if you can imagine an uglier, fatter version of Shirley Booth.
jokes.setup: George trying to be seductive by sharing personal story
jokes.punchline: Completely inappropriate oversharing about his disgusting mother
jokes.explanation: Perfect George moment showing his complete inability to read social situations and tendency to overshare inappropriately.

jokes.text: Hey, your mother's uglier than Hazel. Hazel really puts your mother to shame.
jokes.punchline: Specific, absurd insults comparing his mother unfavorably to a TV character
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up that continues the inappropriate oversharing with specific, vivid details.

jokes.text: Eight dollars? What are you doing, robbing me? I wasted my whole morning on you for $8?
jokes.setup: George thinks he's about to have romantic encounter
jokes.punchline: Woman reveals she's a prostitute upset about low payment
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection that completely reframes the entire subway encounter. Great shock value and character humiliation.

jokes.text: You can't just leave me here. Will I see you again?
jokes.setup: Woman is leaving with George's clothes
jokes.punchline: George's pathetic plea despite being robbed and humiliated
jokes.explanation: Perfect capstone to George's humiliation showing his desperate neediness even in disaster.

jokes.text: Kramer's horse racing victory celebration
jokes.punchline: Kramer's over-the-top celebration and vindication
jokes.explanation: Satisfying payoff to Kramer's horse betting subplot with characteristic enthusiasm.

jokes.text: Hey, hey! Give me the money. Freeze! Police!
jokes.setup: Kramer is about to be mugged after winning big
jokes.punchline: Police intervention at perfect moment
jokes.explanation: Convenient but satisfying resolution that protects Kramer's windfall.

jokes.text: Hare Krishna! Hare Krishna! How'd you like a Hare Krishna fist down your throat, you little punk?
jokes.punchline: George's violent threat using religious phrase
jokes.explanation: Brilliant juxtaposition of peaceful religious chanting with violent threat. Perfect George rage moment.

jokes.text: Biff.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's simple callback to Death of a Salesman reference
jokes.explanation: Perfect economical callback that brings the episode full circle with one word.

jokes.text: I'm sitting on the D train for an hour and 15 minutes to go on a scary ride. You know that first sharp drop on the Cyclone? Fell asleep.
jokes.setup: Jerry went to Coney Island for thrilling ride
jokes.punchline: Fell asleep on the actual ride after long subway journey
jokes.explanation: Perfect closing observation that ties together the episode's themes about urban absurdity and anticlimactic experiences.

tmdb_overview: A beauty picks up George; Jerry makes friends with a naked guy; Kramer is tipped off about a hot horse; Elaine gets stuck en route to a lesbian wedding.
